label: yes

Now, take 4 steps left. If I'm facing north, left would be to the west, which is the negative x-direction. So 4 steps left would take me to (-4, 0). Then take 3 steps forward. Since I'm still facing north, moving forward from (-4,0) by 3 steps would take me to (-4, 3). Next, take 1 step right. If I'm facing north again, right would be east, the positive x-direction. So moving 1 step right from (-4,3) would take me to (-3, 3). Now, where am I? At (-3, 3). The starting point was (0,0). So I'm not back to the start. Then 1 step to the right relative to your forward direction. So yes, that's how I modeled it earlier. So your direction doesn't change. So each left/right is relative to your facing direction. So if you start facing north, left is west, right is east. So the steps are as I calculated: ending up at (-3,3). So the answer is No, you don't return to the starting point. Wait, but maybe there's another interpretation. Suppose "always face forward" means that after moving left or right, you adjust your facing direction. But that doesn't make sense. Wait, but in standard navigation terms, when you take steps left or right, you are moving relative to your current facing direction. For example, if you're facing north and take a step left, you move west. If you then take a step right, you move east. So the direction you're facing doesn't change; the movement is relative to your current direction. So in that case, the answer would be no, as per my previous calculation.
